item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Sommes-nous refroidissons encore?

Avoir un +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: totally glad to bump this up to 100, macabre and cool.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Hmmmm, I'm not against this one, but I'm also not all for it. Sort of feels like, there's an ending, a climax, that's missing? Something all the weirdness and screenings has built up to? IDK, no voting for me.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I forgot that I removed that. Re-add'd.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Any specific reason the author as Marcel Sabourin and copyright "circa quartam" bits got removed? I quite liked the detail there. EDIT: To be specific, the copyright date is what's gone now and what I think was a touch of "just what a dude like that would fill in there"

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
This is pretty much a classic article. A crazy film that sucks you in, Cocteau-style. I totally expected a French origin; the "scene removed" and the redaction, along with all other scenes, are great.

+1, great.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: So, it's not about Sabourin, but Lepage, and there is no reference or allusion to AWCY?, but I did finally finish the story.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
1605: Now with more [DATA EXPUNGED]!

I like to think it's the fairy of creepy [DATA EXPUNGED], rather than the bullshit fairy of lazy [DATA EXPUNGED], but only time will tell.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: How had I not upvoted this until now? I love it.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Noted and fixed. I removed the bit about the researcher, as it didn't add much, and made it into a silent film.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
So? If you write it up it doesn't mean it's the canon origin of it. Hell, SCP-914 has at least two entirely contradictory origin stories.

Please do finish it off.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Oh ho ho, how the tables have turned in the past two months.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
It is theorized that by glancing at the film during his work with it, he had accidentally viewed it in its entirety.

Do you glancing at the film on the projector screen, or glancing at the physical film itself (i.e. while loading/unloading it into the projector)?

I ask because of this:

When watched in its entirety by a sapient being capable of processing audio-visual stimuli

The audio part of "audio-visual" seems to imply (to me) that the person has to be capable of hearing the film, which would have been impossible in the latter case. They also have not done a test with a deaf person to see if this makes a difference.

However, I do like the fact that they tested it on a person with synesthesia. You don't see that often on items only affect people who experience it with a specific sense. I don't know if I typed that sentence in a way that makes sense.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: After reading this, I got about halfway through writing a tale about how Sabourin inspired AWCY. But the combination of writing about my own article/giving an origin to a group that isn't mine felt a bit weird/like bad form. However, if someone else feels like writing something, feel free…

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Maybe Marcel Sabourin was a founding member, or better, the inspiration for AWCY. Either way, this deserves a tale at the very least.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This. The concept isn't that imteresting, but the film's description gave me shivers.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Centipede boobs seem to be a popular choice.

I was also considering trying to old-film-ify/censor the face of the patient of the following and have it be a still from 1:52
http://historymatters.gmu.edu/d/6669/

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I liked the description of the surrealist film too, although that might be to do with my fondness for watching odd films in the small hours of the morning whilst under the influence of something!
Considering creating a snapshot of the film somehow with Photoshop, any suggestions for a particular scene?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
1) I added an experiment log making it clear that it's only the film itself, not recordings, that have weird effects. Presumably, lab workers only view recordings of the film. If you mean how do they keep from being absorbed during testing, they project it from a separate room and don't watch.
2) They appear as themselves until they are killed (which happens a frequently) or go insane to the point that their actions are indistinguishable from the "actual" characters.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
When watched by a sentient being capable of processing audio-visual stimuli, the viewer (hereafter, "the subject") disappears and their likeness appears in the film.

1) It should say "sapient" instead of "sentient", unless you mean that things like dogs and cats can be absorbed into it.

2) It should say that the film must be watched in it's entirety for the subject to be absorbed.

3) It should say whether or not direct viewing is required for the effect to happen. If indirect viewing protects against the effect, it should probably say that only D-class may view it directly, and researchers must view it indirectly.

Intertitle stating "Scène Supprimée Par Ordre De" (Scene Removed By Order Of). The names of the subjects in the film are then listed.

It'd be amusing if is said "Scene Removed By The Order of O5-X", where "X" was a different number on each viewing.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Wow, that's really cool. I was considering making a few other art-based SCPs, all centered around "Marcel Sabourin," but it looks like I got beaten to the chase. Plus, dangerous guerilla art is much more threatening!

But this doesn't seem like their sort of thing. I could definitely see them taking inspiration from it, though.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
The concept seems a bit stale to me, though I do like the movie description. I find that genuinely creepy.

The humorous addenda ruin it though.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: AWCY is a Group of Interest consisting of an indeterminate number of individuals who use SCP-level objects to commit guerilla art attacks. Not a lot has been written with them yet.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
This definitely has their vibe, but the film is too old.

…Or at least it SEEMS to be…

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Are we cool yet?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This is such a strong and creepy image, probably the best of the bunch.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: It's halfway decent…. But now I really, really want to see actual footage of a bunch of people chasing down a carriage while throwing cabbages at it, only to suddenly all stop and begin waltzing. Then get chased away by a bunch of becarpeted bulls charging out of nowhere.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Eh. With more work I could really like this, but as it is I don't really care for it. The "D-Class Movie Night" thing in particular kind of irks me. No vote.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This is pretty good. I like the description of the events in the film, but I have a few questions. First, how are they able to view it safely for the tests? Second, does everyone absorbed into the film permanently appear as a character?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Excellent re-write. Hopefully the scp will go positive soon.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Much better with the new details. Downvote changed to upvote.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I think FuzzyBoots has the right idea here. The note about in-film deaths would be extremely interesting if previous audiences were kept inside the film until their on-screen deaths.

As you said, the timing of the absorption and appearance of test subjects could be made more explicit. I imagine that the subjects could be taken into the film sometime later after the film is finished. Like, they disappear in their sleep or something. But the point in the film where the audience disappears should definitely be made more clear. (Right after the film, on the last scene, days later?)

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Remembers me of the man absorbing phone but couldn't really like it
Edit:You did a good job re-writing this. Upvote.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I like that this is sort of the reverse Ring. Getting stuck IN the video is definitely scarier than a ghost girl jumping out of the TV any day. I'm going to upvote this because even though it's little barren, it has potential.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
When the chest is uncovered, two large holes, filled with centipedes, are revealed in place of her breasts.

You can go somewhere with this.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I actually like this one, just on account of the film being so bizarre and nonsensical that being trapped in it sounds horrifying. It's like getting stuck in the film from the Ring.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Very good points, I'll definitely add research notes.

The more that I think about it, the more I like your idea about people adding to the film length rather than getting deleted. Beyond making it more interesting (a growing film, rather than one that's a set length), it totally seems like something a conceptual artist with unimaginable power would do.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I kind of like the idea of it, but it does feel a bit bare. The description of the film's contents suddenly ends with an intertitle. Is that all the film that's there? If not, I feel like the researchers would indicate something about what happens after (if it's just blank film, darkness, but they'd note that). Also, it feels a bit odd for them to stop with "if you watch all of the film, you're sucked in". Given the way the Foundation is generally shown trying to experiment, I'd prefer to see something along the lines of "After watching approximately 11.3 minutes of the movie's footage, the subject is transported to the movie. Note to researchers, this is 11.3 cumulative minutes. Repeated exposure will result in adverse effects" or "If the entirety of the twelfth segment is viewed, the viewer is transported into the movie". Also, it's fairly standard in objects which do something when you observe them that the Foundation notes whether recordings have the same effect, if viewing it through a camera has the same effect, and whether it affects people with given vision issues.

I do like the description of the surreal imagery, although the nature of the film seems to be somewhat self-limiting. Would it work if every group sucked in on a viewing created a new segment with additional film? That would produce a reason to continue studying the film (presumably using D-class or viewing it via camera if such removes the effect) to try to determine who the trapped people are.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
It's a fair idea to start with, but it needs expansion.
And good spelling/grammar… and it could use a bit more depersonalization.
There's just nothing that pulls me in.

All in all, I was intrigued by the concept, disappointed by the execution.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Way too short, no hook, poorly written. Downvote.

EDIT: Very much improved. +1.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: If it's surrealist, wouldn't it make more sense to use a duck or something?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Well, it IS a surrealist film…

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Literally, with small hammers?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Agreed that there's just not enough "there" there. Perhaps expand on what the actual content of the film is? Can it absorb more than one person at a time (multiple people viewing it at the same time)? Perhaps expand it a little such that the original copy traps individuals, but copies of it have the trapped individuals become gradually aware that they've been trapped and start breaking the 4th wall?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Too bare bones. Decently written, but the idea isn't interesting.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Sorry, but… it's really got no hook. I can't bring myself to like it.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Get rid of the name and just say something about asking senior site staff?

I was also considering adding a short section about it being found either following an incident at a film festival, or retrieved from the archives of a collector.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Aside from the clichédness of a film that sucks you in, this article seems sort of too short and barren. Also, names in the Containment Procedures are one of my major pet peeves; SCPs do not 'belong to' individuals, nor is access ever controlled by a single individual.
